quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Definite integral"?

options.text: f'(x) represents the instantaneous rate of change of whatever f(x) models: population, cost, temperature, concentration
State the integral being evaluated, show the antiderivative, substitute limits, and simplify; credit for setup even if computation errors occur
A point where concavity changes; f''(x) = 0 or undefined AND the sign of f''(x) changes
∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx; the signed area between f(x) and the x-axis from x = a to x = b

quiz_questions.explanation: "Definite integral" is defined as: ∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx; the signed area between f(x) and the x-axis from x = a to x = b. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Riemann sum"?

options.text: State the theorem precisely, verify hypotheses, apply the conclusion, and interpret in the context of the problem
F(x) = ∫ₐˣ f(t)dt; represents the accumulated area from a to x; its derivative is f(x) by FTC Part 1
All variables are functions of time, so d/dt[x²] = 2x·dx/dt, d/dt[xy] = x·dy/dt + y·dx/dt
An approximation of the definite integral using n rectangles: Σf(xᵢ*)Δx, where xᵢ* is the sample point in each subinterval

quiz_questions.explanation: "Riemann sum" is defined as: An approximation of the definite integral using n rectangles: Σf(xᵢ*)Δx, where xᵢ* is the sample point in each subinterval. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Left/Right/Midpoint sum"?

options.text: Left-hand limit: lim(x→c⁻) f(x); right-hand limit: lim(x→c⁺) f(x); the two-sided limit exists only if both one-sided limits are equal
Find dy/dt given dx/dt (or vice versa) by differentiating an equation relating x and y with respect to time t
f''(x) > 0; the graph curves upward like a cup; the slope is increasing
Left: sample at left endpoint; Right: sample at right endpoint; Midpoint: sample at center of each subinterval

quiz_questions.explanation: "Left/Right/Midpoint sum" is defined as: Left: sample at left endpoint; Right: sample at right endpoint; Midpoint: sample at center of each subinterval. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Fundamental Theorem (Part 1)"?

options.text: = e^(f(x)) · f'(x); the most common chain rule application with exponentials
If F(x) = ∫ₐˣ f(t)dt, then F'(x) = f(x); differentiation undoes integration
In economics: marginal cost C'(x), marginal revenue R'(x), and marginal profit P'(x) = R'(x) - C'(x) at production level x
A point where the concavity of f changes; requires f''(x) = 0 or undefined AND a sign change in f''

quiz_questions.explanation: "Fundamental Theorem (Part 1)" is defined as: If F(x) = ∫ₐˣ f(t)dt, then F'(x) = f(x); differentiation undoes integration. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Antiderivative"?

options.text: A function F(x) whose derivative is f(x): F'(x) = f(x); the general antiderivative includes + C
[f(b)-f(a)]/(b-a); the average rate of change over [a,b]; the slope of the line connecting endpoints
From a graph, check if the function approaches the same value from the left and right; if not, the two-sided limit does not exist
d/dx[xⁿ] = nxⁿ⁻¹ for any real n; includes x⁻¹ = 1/x, x^(1/2) = √x, x^(2/3), etc.

quiz_questions.explanation: "Antiderivative" is defined as: A function F(x) whose derivative is f(x): F'(x) = f(x); the general antiderivative includes + C. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx; the signed area between f(x) and the x-axis from x = a to x = b"?

options.text: Bounding function changes
F increasing/decreasing
Definite integral
Integration by parts formula

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Definite integral".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "An approximation of the definite integral using n rectangles: Σf(xᵢ*)Δx, where xᵢ* is the sample point in each subinterval"?

options.text: Related rates setup
Riemann sum
MVT with restrictions
Exponential function

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Riemann sum".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Left: sample at left endpoint; Right: sample at right endpoint; Midpoint: sample at center of each subinterval"?

options.text: Linear approximation
Justification language
Left/Right/Midpoint sum
Tangent lines to implicit curves

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Left/Right/Midpoint sum".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "If F(x) = ∫ₐˣ f(t)dt, then F'(x) = f(x); differentiation undoes integration"?

options.text: Second derivative test
Units of derivative
Fundamental Theorem (Part 1)
Interval additivity

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Fundamental Theorem (Part 1)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A function F(x) whose derivative is f(x): F'(x) = f(x); the general antiderivative includes + C"?

options.text: Logistic DE
Net change interpretation
Antiderivative
Table-based accumulation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Antiderivative".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Introduction to Integrals is accurate?

options.text: Introduction to Integrals presents integration as the reverse of differentiation and as the accumulation of quantities.
The fundamental pair: d/dx[sin x] = cos x and d/dx[cos x] = -sin x (derived from the limit definition using lim(h→0) sin(h)/h = 1).
Formal Definition of Limits (Epsilon-Delta) provides the rigorous mathematical foundation for limits.

The method: (1) identify the quantity to optimize, (2) express it as a function of one variable using constraints, (3) find critical points where f'(x) = 0, (4) verify maxima/minima using the first or second derivative test, (5) check endpoints...

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Introduction to Integrals as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Introduction to Integrals?

options.text: When k > 0: exponential growth (population, investment); when k < 0: exponential decay (radioactive decay, drug clearance).
Continuous compound interest follows A = Pe^(rt).
The definite integral ∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx represents the signed area between f(x) and the x-axis from a to b.
Key consequences include: (1) if f'(x) = 0 for all x in (a,b), then f is constant, (2) if f'(x) = g'(x) for all x, then f(x) = g(x) + C, (3) if f'(x) > 0 on (a,b), then f is strictly increasing.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Introduction to Integrals as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Introduction to Integrals?

options.text: Key properties: ∫ₐᵇ [f ± g]dx = ∫ₐᵇ f dx ± ∫ₐᵇ g dx (linearity); ∫ₐᵇ cf dx = c∫ₐᵇ f dx (constant multiple); ∫ₐᵇ f dx + ∫ᵇᶜ f dx = ∫ₐᶜ f dx (additivity over intervals); ∫ₐᵃ f dx = 0; ∫ₐᵇ f dx = -∫ᵇₐ f dx (reversing limits).

The method: (1) identify the quantity to optimize, (2) express it as a function of one variable using constraints, (3) find critical points where f'(x) = 0, (4) verify maxima/minima using the first or second derivative test, (5) check endpoints...
A slope field (direction field) displays short line segments at grid points (x,y), each with slope equal to f(x,y) from dy/dx = f(x,y).
Riemann sums approximate this area using rectangles: left, right, and midpoint sums with n subintervals.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Introduction to Integrals as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Introduction to Integrals?

options.text: These are important for integration and solving applied problems.
If f'(x) > 0 on an interval, then f is increasing on that interval; if f'(x) < 0, f is decreasing.
As n → ∞, Riemann sums converge to the exact integral.
Continuity and Types of Discontinuity classifies discontinuities into three types.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Introduction to Integrals as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Introduction to Integrals?

options.text: The Mean Value Theorem for Integrals guarantees there exists a c in [a,b] where f(c) = f_avg—the function actually achieves its average value at some point.
Implicit Differentiation finds dy/dx when y is not explicitly solved as a function of x.
The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus connects differentiation and integration: d/dx[∫ₐˣ f(t)dt] = f(x).
These derivatives are essential for modeling growth, decay, and natural phenomena.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Introduction to Integrals as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Introduction to Integrals?

options.text: Calculating distance traveled from a velocity-time graph: the area under the curve equals total displacement, used in trip planning and vehicle testing
AP Calculus free-response questions consistently test the ability to set up integrals from verbal descriptions and geometric reasoning
Orbital mechanics: computing orbital periods and trajectories involves integrals with expressions like √(a²-x²), solved with trigonometric substitution
Rocket propulsion: as fuel is burned, the rocket becomes lighter, changing the force equation—integration handles the continuously varying mass

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Introduction to Integrals.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Introduction to Integrals?

options.text: Computing total energy consumed over time: integrating power (watts) over time gives energy (joules), essential for electrical grid management
Financial mathematics: differentiating revenue functions R(x) to find marginal revenue and determine the optimal quantity to maximize profit
Modeling the trajectory of a projectile with air resistance: the implicit equation relating position components requires combined chain rule and implicit differentiation
Estimating total water consumption from flow rate measurements taken at regular intervals—a practical Riemann sum application in resource management

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Introduction to Integrals.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Introduction to Integrals applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Acceleration as the second derivative of position: a car speeding up has positive s''(t), slowing down has negative s''(t), and constant speed has s''(t) = 0
Population modeling: P(t) = P₀e^(rt), so P'(t) = rP₀e^(rt) = rP(t), showing exponential growth rate is proportional to population size
Estimating total rainfall from rate-of-rainfall data: ∫(rainfall rate)dt gives total accumulated precipitation over a storm event
Algorithm analysis in computer science: O(n log n) sorting algorithms (merge sort) outperform O(n²) algorithms (bubble sort) for large datasets

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Introduction to Integrals.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Introduction to Integrals?

options.text: Calculating distance traveled from a velocity-time graph: the area under the curve equals total displacement, used in trip planning and vehicle testing
Modeling the trajectory of a projectile with air resistance: the implicit equation relating position components requires combined chain rule and implicit differentiation
Compound interest: computing d/dn[(1 + r/n)^(nt)] using logarithmic differentiation to analyze how compounding frequency affects growth
Financial mathematics: differentiating revenue functions R(x) to find marginal revenue and determine the optimal quantity to maximize profit

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Introduction to Integrals.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Introduction to Integrals in practice?

options.text: Computing total energy consumed over time: integrating power (watts) over time gives energy (joules), essential for electrical grid management
Submarine hull design: calculating pressure at operating depth determines hull thickness needed to prevent collapse
Speedometer readings as instantaneous velocity: the derivative of position with respect to time at each moment, computed electronically in modern vehicles
Total rainfall accumulation: if r(t) is the rainfall rate at time t, then total accumulated rainfall by time x is R(x) = ∫₀ˣ r(t)dt

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Introduction to Integrals.
